coredata.dc:title: Sustainable fabrication of N-doped carbon quantum dots and their applications in fluorescent inks, Fe (III) detection and fluorescent films
coredata.dc:creator: Zhai, Zhirou
Xu, Jiali
Gong, Tianyu
Cao, Bingqiang
Cui, Kai
Hou, Linrui
Yuan, Changzhou
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the highly fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method by using garlic skins (GSs) as the sole carbon source. The resultant N-doped CQDs with excitation-dependent emission demonstrates remarkable quantum yield of ∼9% and superior fluorescence stability. Their strong cyan-emission character upon irradiation with UV light (365 nm) reveals their huge potential in anticounterfeit field as the fluorescent ink. Owing to the fluorescence quenching resulted from the tightly chelating of Fe3+ to the surface of N-doped CQDs, the CQDs can be applied a functional sensor for the detection of Fe3+, and the limit of detection is estimated as 4.44 μM. Furthermore, the CQDs is dispersed into poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) matrix to make PVA/CQDs composite film. The unique optical properties of CQDs are inherited by the PVA/CQDs film, giving intensive cyan fluorescence and high visible transparency, as well as nearly 100% UV-blocking ratio in the UV region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
N doping
Fluorescent ink
Fluorescent sensor
Fluorescent hybrid film

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (the inset) images, (b) size distribution histogram and (c) XRD pattern of the CQDs.
(a) Full XPS spectrum, and high-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of the CQDs.

(a) The UV absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs. (b) PL spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 390 nm. (c) The digital photographic images of a filter paper with a hand-written Chinese character meaning “light” (光) exposed to (c) the daylight light and (d) 365 nm UV light. The insets in panel (a) for the digital photographs of the CQDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation.

PL spectra and normalized PL intensities of CQDs (a, b) upon irradiation by a UV lamp with the centre wavelength at 365 nm, (c, d) in different ionic strength, (e, f) within a temperature range from 25 to 45 °C, and (g, h) after different storage time in air at RT (λex = 365 nm).
(a) PL spectra and (b) normalized PL intensities of the CQDs in aqueous solutions with different pH values from 1 to 12 (λex = 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence response of QDs towards different metal ions of 1 mM, and (b) F/F0 with various metal ions as indicated.
F 0
and
F
for the fluorescence intensities before and after addition of the metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of various Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM. (b) The linear relationship of F/F0 versus the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0 – 100 μM. F0 and F for the fluorescence intensity before and after addition of Fe3+.
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the CQDs solutions with and without Fe3+ ion as indicated.
Digital photograph of PVA/CQDs film under the irradiation of (a) visible light and (b) 365 nm UV light. (c - f) Digital photographs of PVA/CQDs film with different conditions.
(a) UV–Vis transmittance and (b) absorption spectra of PVA/CQDs and PVA films, (b) PL emission spectra of PVA/CQDs film excited with different excited wavelengths from 340 to 430 nm.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CQDs from garlic skin towards the detection of Fe3+.
Schematic representation of the fabrication of PVA/CQDs films.
The comparison of the current work with the Fe3+ determination in other reported works.

head.ce:title: Sustainable fabrication of N-doped carbon quantum dots and their applications in fluorescent inks, Fe (III) detection and fluorescent films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped CQDs are efficiently fabricated with a green synthesis avenue, and exhibit huge potential in anticounterfeit application as the fluorescent ink, a smart fluorescent sensor towards ionic detection, and fluorescent polymer films.

In this work, the highly fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method by using garlic skins (GSs) as the sole carbon source. The resultant N-doped CQDs with excitation-dependent emission demonstrates remarkable quantum yield of ∼9% and superior fluorescence stability. Their strong cyan-emission character upon irradiation with UV light (365 nm) reveals their huge potential in anticounterfeit field as the fluorescent ink. Owing to the fluorescence quenching resulted from the tightly chelating of Fe3+ to the surface of N-doped CQDs, the CQDs can be applied a functional sensor for the detection of Fe3+, and the limit of detection is estimated as 4.44 μM. Furthermore, the CQDs is dispersed into poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) matrix to make PVA/CQDs composite film. The unique optical properties of CQDs are inherited by the PVA/CQDs film, giving intensive cyan fluorescence and high visible transparency, as well as nearly 100% UV-blocking ratio in the UV region.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of fluorescence nanomaterial, have received great interests due to their benign and inexpensive nature, as well as wide applications in optoelectroic, photocatalysis, and biological fields [1–3]. In comparison to traditional photoluminescent (PL) materials, such as semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, the PL CQDs demonstrate their incomparable advantages of low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high resistance to photobleaching, and good aqueous solubility. Thus, the CQDs are considered as the most promising candidate for the fluorescent applications.

Up to now, a series of methods, including microwave-assisted synthesis [4], solid phase method [5], heating decomposition [6], wet chemistry route [7], ultrasonic method [8], laser ablation [9], electrochemical synthesis [10], hydrothermal method [11] and sovolthermal method [12], etc. have been developed to synthesize PL CQDs. Among these methods, hydrothermal method is becoming a popular yet efficient route due to its low cost, scalable preparation and facile manipulation [11]. Besides, various types of carbon sources, such as chemical products [13,14], industrial products [15], minerals [16], and biomass [17], and even some waste residues [18–20], have been applied to synthesize the PL CQDs. Especially, the considerable efforts have been recently paid on the synthesis of the heteroatom-doped CQDs due to their improved PL properties, like enhanced PL quantum yield (QY), stronger resistance to self-quenching, higher sensing electivity [21,22]. Although great advances have been achieved in the synthetic techniques and starting materials, the simpler, green and sustainable avenues are still highly desirable to produce heteroatom-doped CQDs with high PL quality, and even expand wider applications.

In recent years, the increasing counterfeit goods in medicines, food products, and currency have caused serious economic losses and even health risks to human beings. Thus, it is highly urgent to exploit the innovative and scalable anticounterfeiting and security technologies to identify counterfeiting. So far, various anti-counterfeiting technologies have been applied to address these issues, such as stealth technology, laser holograms, and fluorescent inks, and so on [23,24]. Among them, the fluorescent ink has achieved the unprecedented development due to its more convenient manipulation. As the result, the stimuli-responsive fluorescent materials have become the research focus of the fluorescent inks.

In addition, the CQDs-based photoluminescent (PL) sensors have also received much attention due to their potential applications in the highly sensitive and selective detection of metal ions [25,26]. Iron is known to all as an important and indispensable trace element in living organisms [27–29]. Especially, the deficiency and overloading of iron can both disturb the cellular homeostasis and lead to various biological disorders [30]. Therefore, it is necessary and desirable to detect Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. Recently, CQDs have been reported for the detection of Fe3+. For instance, A.Q. Hassan etc. synthesized dual emission polymer carbon dots which showed great potential in Fe3+ detection [31]. L.J. Mohammed etc. synthesized CQDs with high selectivity for the detection of Fe3+ by hydrothermal treatment [32]. K.M. Omer etc. prepared phosphorus/nitrogen doped CQDs with good selectivity for the detection of Fe3+ via a hydrothermal method [33]. But it is still desirable to develop more efficient CQDs nanoprobe with low cost, simple process and great practicability [34].

In this work, a one-step hydrothermal strategy was developed for the green synthesis of N-doped CQDs from cheap, renewable and natural waste garlic skins (GSs), where no any extra additives as well as organic solvents were applied here. GSs, as the waste residue of garlics, was chosen as the sole carbon source to prepare N-doped CQDs. The obtained water-soluble N-doped CQDs exhibited a strong cyan luminescence with a high QY of ∼9%, excitation wavelength dependent emission and outstanding fluorescence stability. More appealingly, our CQDs showed huge potential in anticounterfeit application as the fluorescent ink, and in the highly sensitive and selective determination of cations as a smart fluorescent sensor. Furthermore, by incorporating the resulted CQDs into poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) film, the fluorescent hybrid polymer films (PVA/CQDs) were prepared. Meanwhile, the enhanced UV-blocking and UV-absorption properties of PVA/CQDs film were further evaluated.

Ultra-pure (UP) water used throughout all experiments was deionized water purified by using a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore). NaCl, KCl, FeCl3, CrCl3⸱H2O, CuCl2 H2O, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2 6H2O and FeSO4 7H2O were all obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, P. R. China). Garlic was purchased from a local Hualian Supermarket of Jinan in Shandong province, P. R. China. The aqueous solutions of metal ions were prepared from either their sulfate or chloride. All chemicals used in the work were of analytical grade and used directly as received without any further purification.

CQDs were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, as displayed in Scheme 1 . Briefly, 0.1 g of the splintered GSs and 35 mL of UP water were transferred into a Teflon-equipped autoclave (50 mL) and retained at 200 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature (RT), the supernatant solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. Then, the obtained solution was filtered again, and purified through a membrane filter (0.22 μm). Finally, the CQDs powder was obtained via vacuum freeze drying process at −80 °C for 48 h and preserved in refrigerator (4 °C), which can be redissolved in UP water for the further characterization and applications. The stock solution of CQDs (0.50 mg/mL) was prepared by again dissolving 5.00 mg of the reserved CQDs powder in 10 mL of UP water.

The fluorescence QY of CQDs was determined by a comparison method [35] with quinine sulfate (PLQY = 54%, excited at 360 nm) as standard reference as following procedure. For the purpose of minimizing self-absorption effect, the absorbance of the solution of the reference sample and the suspensions of CQDs were controlled below 0.10 by dilution. The relative QY can be calculated through the following equation [36].
Q
Y x
= Q
Y
st
I x
A
st
A x
I
st
η x
η st
2
where I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A refers to the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The subscript ‘‘st” and ‘‘x” refer to quinine sulfate and CQDs, respectively. For all these solutions, η x/η st is approximately equal to 1.

By loading the solution of CQDs into a fountain pens, we wrote the Chinese character meaning “light”(光) on filter paper, and took photos under the irradiation of daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively.

The CQDs solution with a mass concentration of -0.50 mg/mL was chosen to explore its selectivity towards various metal ions under identical conditions at RT. Meanwhile, nine stock solutions (5 mM) separately containing metal ions of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ were prepared from their salts. Typically, 1 mL of each salt solution was mixed with 3 mL of CQDs solution by gentle shaking. All the experiments were conducted under the same conditions, and the relevant fluorescence spectra were recorded after reaction for 5 min at RT. As for the sensitivity of CQDs towards Fe3+, the operation was performed via the same procedure with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (1 mL) into 3 mL of CQDs solution (0.50 mg/mL), and the concentrations of Fe3+ varied from 0 to 100 mM. The excited wavelength was set at 365 nm.

The PVA/CQDs films were moulded as exhibited in Scheme 2 . PVA granules (2 g) was firstly dispersed in UP water (18 mL) under constant stirring. Then, the mixture was heated to 80 °C and then maintained at 80 °C for 1 h to dissolve PVA. Afterwards, 2 mL of CQDs aqueous solution (0.50 mg/mL) was mixed with 18 mL of PVA aqueous solution and further stirred for 20 min. Finally, the resultant mixed solution was transferred into a clean Petri dish and dried in an oven at 45 °C for 12 h. After drying, the PVA/CQDs composite films were peeled off from the Petri dish.

The morphology and size of the CQDs were obtained using transmission electron microscope (TEM), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM 2100 system operating at a voltage of 200 kV). The size distribution of N-CQDs was observed by applying a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu Kα radiation, Rigaku Ultima IV) pattern was conducted at a step scan of 15°/min ranging from 5 to 80°. The elemental component analysis of CQDs was performed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, Escalab 250xi spectrometer). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were characterized by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) manufactured by Shimadzu Corporation. The fluorescence spectra were acquired by a RF-6000 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence decay spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS980 instrument.

In the contribution, the N-doped CQDs were prepared via a facile and efficient hydrothermal method by using the GSs as the sole carbon precursor without any additional organic reagents. Generally, the daily great demand for garlic spawns large amounts of garlic skin waste. The GSs, as an easily available and renewableagricultural waste, comprises large amounts of cellulose and nitrogen-containing antioxidants and pectin, and can be an ideal biomass candidate for the synthesis of N-doped CQDs. To obtain the optimal synthetic conditions for the CQDs, the parameters including hydrothermal temperatures and durations, as well as the mass of GSs were performed to explore their influences on PL properties of CQDs, and corresponding results were collected (Fig. S1). Obviously, three hydrothermal parameters only affect the PL intensities not the positions. By comparison, the optimal hydrothermal duration, hydrothermal temperatures and GSs mass are determined as 12 h, 200 °C and 0.10 g, respectively.

The morphology and size of the resultant CQDs prepared under the optimally synthetic condition were characterized by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 1 a). Typical TEM image (Fig. 1a) shows that the CQDs display relatively uniform, nano spherical particles without obvious aggregation. The absence of discernible lattice structures of CQDs on the HRTEM image (the inset in panel a) reveals the amorphous nature of the resultant CQDs. The corresponding statistical particle size distribution histograms displayed in Fig. 1b exhibits that these CQDs have a narrow size distribution ranging from 2.5 to 5.5 nm estimated from the TEM image (Fig. 1a), and the average size of these particles is about 3.55 nm. In addition, the typical XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) demonstrates a broad, low intensity diffraction peak mainly centered at 2θ of ca. 23.54°, suggesting the nanoscale particle size of the CQDs.

Typical XPS technique was also carried out to determine the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the CQDs. The full spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2 a, displays two strong peaks at ∼532.2 and ∼284.8 eV, as well as a weak peak at ∼399.8 eV, corresponding to the O 1s, C 1s and N 1s, respectively, which confirms the presence of O-rich functional groups and N-containing species in the CQDs. And the relative elemental contents are ∼76.83 at.% of C, ∼22.23 at.% of O and ∼0.94 at.% of N, respectively, as evidenced from the elemental analysis (Table S1). The high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s signal (Fig. 2b) shows four peaks at ∼284.6, ∼285.3, ∼286.7 and ∼288.5 eV, representing the CC, CC, CO/CN and CO/CN groups, respectively [37–39]. As for the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), two relative nitrogen species of pyridinic N (399.8 eV) and graphitic N (401.7 eV) are apparent, confirming the successful synthesis of N-doped CQDs [40]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) is deconvoluted in detail into four fitted peaks at ∼530.8 eV, ∼532.1, ∼532.7 and ∼533.7 eV, which are attributed to the OCO, CO, CO/COH/COC and OCO groups, respectively [41,42]. These results also clearly affirm the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic groups on the CQDs surface. This unique feature makes the resultant CQDs excellent compatibility with the water. Actually, the supernatant solution can still remain in a homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation even after standing for 6 months at RT.

The optical properties of the CQDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, and the relevant spectra were shown in Fig. 3 . As exhibited in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis spectrum displays a wide optical absorption peak mainly at the UV region, which probably originates from the π-π* and n-π* electron transitions of the conjugated carbonyl groups (CC/CO) in the CQDs [43]. As regards the PL spectra, the CQDs reveal the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths located at 365 and 437 nm, respectively. The insets in Fig. 3a exhibit the respective digital photographs of the CQDs solutions under the irradiation of daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). Noticeably, the CQDs solution is transparent, clear and light yellow upon the daylight irradiation, while it shows a strong cyan luminescence, further visually exhibiting their cyan fluorescence character. Fig. 3b displays the emission spectra of CQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 390 nm. Obviously, the as-prepared N-CQDs present the common excitation-dependent PL behavior with the maximum emission peak when excited by 365 nm UV light. With the increase in the excitation wavelength from 340 to 390 nm, the emission peak shifts to long wavelengths from 433 to 472 nm, which is similar to most of the fluorescent CQDs reported before [44]. This phenomenon may be associated with the anti-Stokes PL and the complexity of different surface states in CQDs [6,45]. Furthermore, we apply the resultant CQDs as an invisible fluorescent ink due to their unique luminescence in the solid state. Fig. 3c and d show the designable pattern of the as-prepared CQDs inks deposited on a piece of filter paper. Strikingly, there is no visible pattern appearing on the filter paper under the irradiation of daylight light except a shadow (Fig. 3c), although the paper was written a Chinese character meaning light “光” by using the CQDs as the ink. Interestingly, the pattern of cyan Chinese character meaning light “光” is clearly visible to the naked eye on the filter paper when excited by a 365 nm UV light source (Fig. 3d). Especially, even after discarded at normal conditions at RT for 35 days, the filter paper still demonstrates the strong cyan-emissive pattern (Fig. S2). Thus, this signifies its great potential applications in the areas of security and anti-counterfeiting.

Generally, the fluorescence stability of CQDs is particularly important for their wider applications. Therefore, the fluorescent stability of the as-prepared CQDs was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs solution under long-time UV lamp irradiation, high ionic strength, temperature, storage time at RT. Corresponding results were collectively presented in Fig. 4 . Obviously, no significant photobleaching appears even after continuous UV exposure for 6 h (Fig. 4a, b). The surrounding ionic strength of CQDs is monitored by regulating the concentration range of the added NaCl in CQDs solution from 0 to 1.0 M. As presented Fig. 4c, d, when the concentration of NaCl is increased up to 1 M, the PL intensity of CQDs only displays a slight downward tendency, and the decrease ratio of PL intensity is within ∼9.82%. Particularly, the peak characteristic shows scarce changes, which is beneficial to its use in the solution containing NaCl.

Meanwhile, the PL intensity of CQDs decreases with the gradual ascending of temperature. Nonetheless, just ∼3% of intensity degradation is found even when the temperature is increased to 45 from 25 °C (Fig. 4e, f). Furthermore, the CQDs still demonstrate good fluorescence performance even after storage in air at RT for 15 days (Fig. 4g, h). The excellent photostability and chemical stability endow these CQDs with great potentials for practical applications.

To widen the application of CQDs, the influences of pH on their fluorescence properties were also examined with 365 nm excitation at RT for a wide range of pH values from 1 to 12. The pH values of the CQDs solutions were regulated by separately using HCl (for acidic variation) and NaOH (for base variation). As displayed in Fig. 5 , the as-prepared CQDs demonstrate a distinct pH-dependent performance. Interestingly, the PL intensity of CQDs shows an important difference when the pH value is increased from 1 to 12, while there are no obvious changes in the PL positions (Fig. 5a). Particularly, it can be intuitively seen from Fig. 5b that the PL intensity presents gradual enhancement with the pH value up to 5 then decreases, and a significant quenching phenomenon is achieved by varying the pH value from 8 to 12. The protonation and deprotonation of the amino groups on the surface of the CQDs may result in this pH-dependent PL behavior [45–47]. However, the fluorescence intensity does not increase but decreases by reducing the pH values from 4 to 2, which may be ascribed to the damages of superfluous protonic acid to the protonated CQDs [48,49]. Such a result highlights the sensing application of N-CQDs towards pH values in real samples.

Owing to their excellent PL performance, good water solubility, abundant surface functional groups, strong photostability and chemical stability, the as-prepared CQDs can serve as a promising sensing candidate. Especially, the oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs can interact with metal ions to form complexes or chelate due to coordination effect [50–52]. Accordingly, the feasibility of N-CQDs as a prober towards metal ion detection was systemically investigated. The maximum PL intensities of the CQDs solutions with a single additive of representative metal ions (5 mM) under the same conditions, including Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+, are monitored under the same conditions to assess the selectivity of the sensing system. As showed in Fig. 6 a, the PL peak characteristics of CQDs show no remarkable changes with the addition of each metal ion except PL intensity. Compared to other metal ions, Fe3+ ion has the strongest capability to quench the fluorescence of CQDs. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+ may be ascribed to the formation of the nonluminous complex between Fe3+ and oxygen (or nitrogen)-containing groups on the surfaces of CQDs. Fe3+ ion, a universal electron acceptor, contributes to the non-radiative electron transfer process from CQDs to the d orbital of Fe3+ [53,54].

Additionally, the detection sensitivity of CQDs towards Fe3+ is equallyimportant for its application. To find out the detection range, the fluorescence intensity changes of CQDs solutions with the additive different concentrations of Fe3+ were investigated, and corresponding results were depicted in Fig. 7 . Noticeably, the gradual decreasing of PL intensity of CQDs is observed along with the enhancement of Fe3+ concentration in solution, suggesting the sensitive response of CQDs towards the concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. 7a).

Fig. 7b exhibits the relationship between fluorescence response and Fe3+ concentration. There is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.964) between the quenching factor (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0 to 100 μM with a linear equation of
F 0
F
= 1.034 + 0.035
F
e
3 +
, where F0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. With help of Stern-Volmer equation of
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
F
e
3 +
[55], the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (
K
sv
) can be observed to be as 0.035 μM−1. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (
LOD
) is calculated to be 4.44 μM according to the IUPAC three times standard deviation rule (
LOD = 3
σ s
, where σ.is the standard deviation of the blank signals, and s is the slope of the linear calibration curve). The calculated LOD is comparable with those in the previous literature (Table 1 ). Especially, the LOD of our CQDs is lower than the permitted maximum level of Fe3+ in drinking water (5.357 μM) based on the Chinese National Standard [54], further strengthening their high potential application for Fe3+ detection under real conditions.

Generally, the fluorescence lifetime measurement can be applied to explore the fluorescence mechanism of CQDs [53,55,63]. To investigate the fluorescence “on–off” mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ was examined. As shown in Fig. 8 , the lifetime decay curves of the CQDs with and without Fe3+ can be well fitted to three kinetic components with a triexponential function according to the following equation:
Y
x
=
A 1
exp
- x
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
- x
τ 2
+
A 3
exp
- x
τ 3
where A1 , A2 and A3 indicate the corresponding fractional contributions of the time–resolved decay lifetimes of τ1 , τ2 and τ3 . The average lifetime of CQDs is 2.67 ns and can be fitted into three components: A1 = 913.89, τ 1 = 0.35 ns; A2 = 3577.57, τ 2 = 1.81 ns; A3 = 626.30, τ s = 4.79 ns (Table S2). With the addition of Fe3+, the average lifetime of CQDs-Fe3+ changes to 4.86 ns, and such three decay processes consist of three components: A1 = 1531.14, τ 1 = 0.46 ns; A2 = 2821.39, τ 2 = 2.13 ns; A3 = 764.12, τ s = 8.04 ns (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 8, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs is very different for the cases where Fe3+ is absent or present. It exhibits the existence of an ultrafast electron-transfer process in the CQDs-Fe3+ system, suggesting its dynamic quenching mechanism [64]. In other words, the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) is the main cause of the fluorescent quenching of CQDs by the addition of Fe3+.

The solid-state fluorescence of CQDs is especially important for its application in optoelectronic and plastic film fields. Regretfully, the aggregation of the particles commonly results in their fluorescence quenching, thus the fluorescence properties of CQDs in solid state hardly remain remarkable as those in solutions, even appears significant fluorescence quenching [65]. To address the fluorescence quenching, the acceptable and efficient method is to disperse the CQDs in the polymer matrix [60]. The water-soluble PVA is commonly recognized as an excellent matrix for the CQDs due to the good compatibility between CQDs and PVA matrix and outstanding passivation capability of PVA to the surface of CQDs [58,60]. With regard to the above considerations, the PVA/CQDs film was prepared by dispersing the as-prepared CQDs in the PVA matrix.

Fig. 9 a, b exhibit the digital photographs of PVA/CQDs films under the irradiation of visible light and UV light (365 nm), respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 9a, the as-obtained film is highly transparent under the visible irradiation, suggesting the excellent dispersion of CQDs in the PVA matrix. Equally, the solid PVA/CQDs film demonstrates a bright cyan emission upon the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, which is similar to the CQDs solution (Fig. 9b). Particularly, the PVA/CQDs film still presents strong cyan PL property even when it has been placed in air at RT for 56 days (Fig. S3). Such excellent photostability stability of PVA/CQDs film further endows it with great potential for practical applications. Meanwhile, the flexible performance of PVA/CQDs film was examined through fiddling the film with different conditions of floating, one folding, two folding and curling, as evidenced from corresponding digital photographs (Fig. 9c-f). The results indicate the fluorescent film demonstrates good flexibility, which is beneficial to its potential application in real conditions.

In addition, the responsiveness of PVA/CQDs film to the light was also conducted by varying the wavelength range from 200 to 800 nm. Fig. 10 a shows the transmittance spectra of PVA/CQDs and PVA films. Pure PVA film exhibits strong transmittance property at visible light even to the UV light region, and the maximum transmittanceratio is even up to 40% in the visible light region. Interestingly, upon the addition of CQDs into PVA, the PVA/CQDs hybrid film demonstrates much lower transmittance than pure PVA film over the whole UV and visible light region (200–800 nm), and even the transmittance ratios are reduced to nearly zero in the UV light region. Obviously, the addition of CQDs into PVA can greatly improve its UV-blocking property, and such excellent UV-blocking property may be associated with the biomass GS-derived aromatic rings and phenolic hydroxyl groups in CQDs [66], which can strongly absorb UV light. Fig. 10b displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of PVA/CQDs and PVA films. The PVA/CQDs film exhibits much higher absorption property than PVA film in the UV light even extending to the visible light region. Particularly, the absorption for UV light is noticeably enhanced. Meanwhile, the PVA/CQDs film also shows excited wavelength-dependent property like CQDs in aqueous solution (Fig. 10c). These results indicated the CQDs can be a good additive into polymeric materials with improved UV-blocking properties.

In conclusion, the cyan-emitting CQDs were successfully prepared by a facile, ecofriendly and sustainable hydrothermal synthetic method by using the GSs as the sole carbon source without any additives and organic reagents. The as-prepared water-soluble CQDs exhibit good water solubility and excitation-dependent fluorescent behaviors. Especially, the resultant CQDs present high QY (∼9%), excellent fluorescence stability, and long lifetime. The CQDs were utilized as fluorescent ink and sensitive fluorescent detection for Fe3+. The produced CQDs exhibited outstanding sensitivity and selectivity for Fe (III) detection with wide linear range of 0–100 μM. The observed fluorescence quenching may be ascribed to dynamic quenching mechanism. Besides, the CQDs are expanded to prepare the strong fluorescent PVA/CQDs film, and the film demonstrates highly transparent and enhanced UV-blocking and UV-absorption properties. This opens a low-cost and reliable pathway to highly fluorescent biowaste-based CQDs for versatile applications.

Zhirou Zhai: Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiali Xu: Investigation. Tianyu Gong: Investigation. Bingqiang Cao: Formal analysis. Kai Cui: Data curation. Linrui Hou: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Changzhou Yuan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072151, 52171211), Taishan Scholars (No. ts201712050), Jinan Independent Innovative Team (2020GXRC015), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan (XKY2119).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109387.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
